prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call the fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn itself unto a gentle coolness then doth my spirit rise up within me and rejoice

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and their leaves do turn from green unto gold and from gold unto crimson and from crimson unto a deep and burning scarlet

2:4 And the maples do stand forth as pillars of fire upon the hillsides and the oaks do clothe themselves in raiment of bronze and russet and the birches do shimmer like unto beaten gold in the slanting light of the afternoon

2:5 For the sun in that season doth hang lower in the heavens and its light doth come across the land at such an angle that all things are made warm and honeyed in their appearance

2:6 And in the morning there riseth a mist from the valleys and the fields and it doth cling unto the low places like a garment of white linen spread upon the earth

2:7 The air itself doth carry upon it the fragrance of fallen leaves and of apples ripening in the orchards and of wood smoke rising from the chimneys of distant houses

2:8 And the harvest doth come in from the fields even the pumpkins which do sit upon the earth like great orbs of orange and the corn which standeth in shocks and the apples which do bend the boughs of the trees with their abundance

2:9 In the evenings the sky doth declare the glory of the season for it doth burn with colors of purple and amber and rose such as no painter among men could set down upon his canvas

2:10 And the nights do grow longer and the stars do appear earlier in the firmament and they do shine with a brightness and a clarity that the humid airs of summer had hidden from our sight

2:11 There cometh also in that season a stillness upon the land as though the earth itself doth draw a long and peaceful breath before the sleep of winter descends upon it

2:12 And I do love to walk abroad in that season among the fallen leaves which do crackle beneath my feet and to feel the cool wind upon my face and to know that the year is turning in its ancient course

2:13 For autumn doth teach unto us the beauty that dwelleth even in the letting go of things and in the slow and gentle fading of the light it doth whisper unto our hearts that there is grace in every ending

2:14 This then is my testimony concerning the season of autumn that among all the seasons which the Lord hath set in their courses it is the one which speaketh most deeply unto my soul and causeth me to give thanks
